speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape five of the interview with Henry Wiener. The date is February 4, 1996. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] As I-- as I was listening to some of this, it seemed to me that sports played a very big part in your life. It seems that you were always involved either in a Maccabiah or a soccer team.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, there were, because there are not too many [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] sports stars in Jewish history, really, and I felt-- I felt I had probably talent or something, whatever it is. And usually when I was going to high school in Poland, and I was happened to be the only Jew in the class. And at that time, one of the teachers, gym teachers, was conducting a table tennis tournament in the whole school, and I took part in it, and I happened to win the whole tournament. And I was only a sophomore-- no, a freshman. I was a freshman, really, and, I won-- I won the tournament. And I was the only-- the only Jew who took part in it really. All- all the rest was non-Jews. And because until then, really, we were going-- you  were a Jew and you were going to high school, you always had fights. Fights, because you didn't like the comments that the Poles made and all the other things. And-- but after that, after I won the tournaments, I had-- I had many friends, non-Jewish friends. There  was also a reason behind it, because-- you went six days to school in high school. Saturday and every five days, whereby you when you went to Jewish high-- Jewish regular school, you went Sunday. Here we went Saturday.

speaker: interviewer
text: So before this, we went to the-- our rabbi, who was a very learned man. And he was-- name was Dr. Colburg, and he had a [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] from University of-- not University, from-- from Berlin and from Zurich, Switzerland, a very learned man. He was also a Colonol, a chaplain with the Polish army.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he sort of [INAUDIBLE] that, yes, you can-- you can attend school, but you're not allowed to write. That was his passion. So we had to have somebody of our non-Jewish friends and should give us the homework, what they are the one who are writing. And so I had problem-- until then I had problem before I won the tournament, but after I won the tournament, I had many volunteers. So one hand washes the other, so to speak. And after that I didn't have no problems at all as far as-- as far as this was concerned. So in this part, sport helped. And besides, I took part-- besides, when I played for the Maccabi, the team what I played has a second place in Poland. We were-- only we were number two in Poland, and I was-- at that time, I was only 14, 15 years old. So for me, it was a big thing, going, was living in a hotel, and took part in the tournament. I was one of the youngest players, you know, over-- over there. So for me, it was a big thing. But at that time, you're not allowed, really, to belong to a private club in high school under penalty of suspension. Not like today. Over there, if you-- you are on the high school team. On the high school team, you're not allowed to play on a private team. So you had to be very careful what you are doing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And my father really was not aware of all those things with me. My mother is, but not my father. So once-- one time after we-- we had the second place in Poland. There was our pictures in-- in, like, a sports paper type, like type thing. So I showed it to my-- my father. He says, is that's what you went over-- over the weekend? I says, yeah. He says, it's not bad.

speaker: interviewee
text: But when the-- the same picture, my teacher showed me, my gym teacher showed me this-- the one who conducted the tournament. He says, gee, the guy looks like you. But I didn't use my name. I said the name Weirich. Instead of Wiener, Weirich. I says, if not for this that the name is Weirich, I would think 100% it's you. But he knew about this really. You know, he just didn't want to make an issue out of it. So one of the, you know, small things that you remember, you know, in your youth.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now that we've-- it's 50 years after the war, and so much has happened, and you've got past it, is there anything left that you feel you've gained from all of this? Something you want to say to the next generation about all these experiences that you have had come down from it?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Actually, you cannot prepare yourself for this type of a thing, because you have to believe in human nature. You have to believe in human beings. If you don't believe in human beings, then you became a-- a pessimist for the rest of your life and everything that you see in dark glasses. You have to believe in something. You have to believe in human nature, that somewhere along the line there is-- there's life, there's hope, and that's the way we lived. We always lived with hope. During all the terrible times, we always lived with hope that we will be the one who persevere, not our enemies. And this is the main thing I tried to instill in my children, as much as you can. They cannot visualize that-- you cannot see everything in dark glasses. I don't care how bad it is, there is always light on the other side of the tunnel, and we're trying to-- trying to do this. Just look at us. Look at Mom. Look at me. That-- that's-- that's what life is all about.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to thank you, Mr. Wiener, for sharing your story with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you very much. It was a pleasure. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] This is my dear wife of 50 years, Sally, and the products of our life, so to speak, quote, unquote. On my left in the back, the tallest one is my son, Jack, his wife, Tina. In front of her is her-- their daughter, Ashley, and the blonde one here is Olivia. Next to him is my other son, Mark, his wife, Lisa, my oldest grandson-- grandchild-- Joshua.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hello.

speaker: interviewee
text: On my left, on my behind-- right behind me is grand-- my son-in-law, Steven, my daughter, Judy. And in front of her is her oldest one, Dakota, Cody, we call him, and this is Tyler in front of him. And sitting on my lap is Brian, is my son's Mark son. And this is Asia, my daughter's daughter, and on-- on my-- Sally's lap is the great one. What's your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Danny.

speaker: interviewee
text: What's your name? What?

speaker: interviewee
text: Danny.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, Danny. That's Danny. Now we know who he is. For a while I forgot his name. This is the presentation of our great family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jack? [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, I'll speak.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll speak then. I'm the daughter-in-law, and I am also a child of two Holocaust survivors, and it is a great pleasure and an honor to be a part of this family and to see that from these two people, we got these many, many, and that because they were able to survive and continue that there will be many more generations. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, Ashley, go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm really-- I'm very, very happy that my grandparents survived the Holocaust. Hashem gave us a big miracle.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very nice.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very nice.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, who are you? How about you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: No? Well, how about you? Why not? You don't want to say nothing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Josh, do you want to say anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: What is it?

speaker: interviewee
text: What about?

speaker: interviewee
text: What is it? Mark?

speaker: interviewee
text: About your grandparents.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mark? Or Joshua, what-- what about you? Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I am also very glad that my grandparents survived the Holocaust, because if they didn't, none of us would be here.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. Well--

speaker: interviewee
text: Or most of us wouldn't be here.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Right. [LAUGHTER] Well, Mark?

speaker: interviewee
text: He wants to say something.

speaker: interviewee
text: He? Who is he? Judy, go ahead. Go ahead, Jack. No, no, no, no secrets here. No secrets. Go ahead, Judy.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I'm all right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Go ahead. Just speak-- speak your mind.

speaker: interviewee
text: Just living and being raised with Holocaust survivors was something different, just something different than, you know, the other children in class. That's-- that's--

speaker: interviewee
text: Had grandparents.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you know, there weren't many children in school with me that had Holocaust surviving parents, and it was an honor. It was just an honor to be born and be a first-- first child of a Holocaust survivor.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mark?

speaker: interviewee
text: It just makes you wonder about how many-- how many more people would have been here had the Holocaust never really happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: Go ahead, Tina.

speaker: interviewee
text: Say something, Dad.

speaker: interviewee
text: Tina?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mommy, you say something.

speaker: interviewee
text: Go ahead, Tina. Go, Tina. Shh.

speaker: interviewee
text: Before I came into this family, I really never knew much about the Holocaust. And what I've learned, it's something that's really very hard to even think what they went through, and the kind of people that they are today. They're very special. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Who do we see in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is of my mother and her brother, Henry. The picture was taken prior to her marriage to my father. Must have been circa 1919. I obtained the picture here from my mother's cousin in the United States. She gave me the picture. That's the only picture I have, really, of my-- of my mother when she was still single.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this? Is this her home?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. In photo studio. This type of thing has to be taken in photo studio. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] This picture is of our family prior to birth of my sister. Picture was taken around 1928. I was about seven and my brother was five years old. It was taken-- the picture was in-- in Horsov. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And my parents must have been, that time, in their late 20s. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my family. I am the one-- the tallest in the high school uniform. That's what they wear in Poland. This is my sister, my brother. My parents are on the back. It was taken on a-- by a street photographer on the main street by us. The name of the street was Wolnosc-- it means the freedom street-- in the city of Horsov where we lived. Must have been circa 1934, '33, '34, some-- something like-- like that. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is of our Maccabi Horsov club, a picture of the gym sessions that we have. Once a week we had gym sessions. And because of the nature of the picture, we notified every club member should come, because we will be taking a sort of big picture. And a lot of members that you see here went to the last Maccabiah before the war to Israel, and they remained there, and that they're for their life. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] Most of our members, of course, are not alive, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who are the people in the second row all dressed up?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the officers of-- of-- of the club itself. The president or-- I don't know their duties, but if they are here that means they were officers of-- of-- of the club itself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am on the bottom row, second from the left, and my brother is fourth from the right. [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What do we see here?

speaker: interviewee
text: We see here my wife, Sally's family. Father is in the middle, and her mother's on the right-hand side. Her sister Tanya is on left-hand side. Sally is in the back of it and her brother Mayer is next to her. The picture was a drawing of-- of the original picture what was not usable and was made over here.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this-- the original photograph taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It must have been in 1936 or 1937, in-- in Wisnicz Nowy, where Sally comes from. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of a castle [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in Wisnicz Nowy, where Sally comes from. The castle is, oh, at least 300 or more years old. Was erected by King of Poland. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of our soccer team in Maccabi Horsov. It was taken 1937, '38. I am in the middle row on the right-hand side. On the top row, third from the left, is my cousin Henry [? Munderer. ?] He's not alive anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he the cousin who lived with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. He survived the war, but he died soon after of tuberculosis. He contracted it in the camp. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is a picture of my Sally taken around '38, '39, and in her hometown in Wisnicz Nowy. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is a picture of my brother and my sister taken about 1939 in, also, in Wisnicz where Sally comes from-- Wisnicz Nowy-- around [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] '38, 39-- '39, '40.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one's your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother's on the right-hand side and my sister's in the middle.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your brother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother's name was Maniek, and my sister's name was Emilia. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is a document whereby it states that my name is Wiener, Henry, and I was born, and where I was born, and that I was liberated in Brnenci. It doesn't state, really, by Schindler, but it says in Brnenci. But everybody knew at that time that, where Brnenci was. And this actually enabled me to-- to-- to travel and having-- having identification whereby other-- other documentation follow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who gave you this document?

speaker: interviewee
text: The document's given to us by the township of-- of Brnenci by the Czech government.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did they give it to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right in the camp itself. They give everybody in the camp who was-- who-- who was there still. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This picture is a picture of the soccer team in the DP camp in field by Nuremberg. I am right smack in the center of the picture, sixth from the left or from the right. This was taken in 1945. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This picture was taken in [INAUDIBLE], also 1945. Next to me is my Sally, of course. Next to her is her sister Tanya and her husband Nathan.
